title: Low-Tech Process-Based Restoration Implementation and Monitoring Protocol
author: Weber, Nick
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0

steps.title: The following document outlines a set...
steps.instruction: The following document outlines a set of attributes and survey methods that can be used to support the design, management, and monitoring of process based riverscape restoration projects. The approach draws heavily on the conceptualization of low-tech process-based restoration (LT-PBR) practices presented by Wheaton et al. (

steps.title: and operationalizes those ideas through development...
steps.instruction: and operationalizes those ideas through development of a unified framework for consistently documenting and presenting restoration information throughout a project lifespan. The protocol has been developed with the following intent: • Present an accessible and flexible set of design attributes and monitoring survey protocols that can be consistently applied to the design, implementation, and monitoring of LT-PBR projects. • Develop a flexible data collection and management solution that encourages adoption of the survey protocols by a diverse set of restoration practitioners and that supports common tasks in proposal development, permit acquisition, and fulfillment of land management agency monitoring and reporting requirements. • Advance the science and art of LT-PBR practices by encouraging the adoption of a standardized design, monitoring, and approach for the calculation and interpretation of summary metrics that describe project outcomes and effectiveness. NOTE This version 1 is based on a FileMaker database application, and is a non map-based protocol. That application is still avaialble at: https://github.com/Riverscapes/fmLTPBR through the Version 1.0 release . As of Winter 2023-2024, the authors and other collaborators in the Riverscapes Consortium are working on a revision to this protocol - Version 2, which will be map based and using GIS. The protocol will be an LTPBR action-effectiveness monitoring protocol and can be used to monitor riverscape health. It will facilitate a mix of field-based and desktop-based GIS mapping and quantificaiton of riverscape health indicators and project implementation and design metrics. The protocol will be possible to implement using any GIS, but we are also concurrently releasing a free desktop GIS plugin within QGIS to faciliatate implementation of the protocol: the QGIS Riverscapes Studio . As of this update, the plugin is available as an experimental plugin (0.3.

steps.title: in the QGIS plugin store
steps.instruction: in the QGIS plugin store. When Version 2.0 of the protocol is published and released, Version 1.0 of QRiS will be released and the status switched from experimental to regular. We hope to have these released by Summer 2024.
